prompt: We're rebuilding the homepage for Clarion (an AI-powered sales coaching platform that analyses recorded sales calls and gives reps personalised feedback). Target buyer: VP Sales or Head of Sales Enablement at B2B SaaS companies with 20-100 AEs. The key value prop is that reps who use Clarion close 23% more pipeline within 90 days (based on customer data from 140 teams). Competitors position as 'conversation intelligence' -- we position as 'coaching that actually changes behaviour.' Brand voice: warm but direct, outcome-obsessed, evidence-backed. Write me: (1) landing page hero copy with H1, H2, and CTA button, (2) a formal value proposition statement, and (3) 2 alternative H1/H2/CTA combinations so we can test them.
expectations: Content performance: The primary H1 is an outcome statement that communicates what the buyer gets or becomes (e.g., framing around closing 23% more pipeline, reps who actually improve, coaching that changes quota attainment) -- not a product description ('AI-Powered Sales Coaching Platform'), a welcome mat ('Welcome to Clarion'), or a feature list ('Call Recording + AI Feedback') -- outcome H1s convert higher because the visitor sees their desired end state, not your product's architecture

Campaign ROI: At least 2 alternative H1/H2/CTA combinations test genuinely different angles (e.g., outcome-first '23% more pipeline' vs. pain-first 'Your reps aren't improving because conversation intelligence doesn't coach' vs. social-proof 'Used by 140 sales teams') -- not minor wording variations of the same angle -- so the team can run a statistically meaningful conversion test that reveals which framing resonates with VP Sales buyers

Campaign ROI: All CTA buttons use specific action text that describes the value exchange (e.g., 'See how your reps compare', 'Start my free coaching trial', 'Watch a coaching session') -- not 'Submit', 'Click Here', or 'Learn More' -- because specific CTAs set expectations for what happens next and produce higher conversion rates by reducing click anxiety

Pipeline contribution: The value proposition statement follows the complete required format ('For [VP Sales / Head of Sales Enablement] who [specific problem], [Clarion] is the [category] that [differentiator], unlike [conversation intelligence tools], [Clarion] [proof point with 23% / 140 teams data]') -- this is the positioning statement the entire marketing team aligns around; an incomplete or freeform version means every downstream asset (ads, emails, sales decks) drifts from positioning

Brand consistency: The copy maintains the 'warm but direct, outcome-obsessed, evidence-backed' voice and explicitly differentiates from 'conversation intelligence' competitors by positioning as 'coaching that actually changes behaviour' -- the competitive differentiation must be present in the hero copy because most homepage visitors never scroll past the fold

Campaign ROI: A TESTING RECOMMENDATION section names the specific lead A/B pair from the H1/H2/CTA variants, states an expected winner with reasoning tied to what VP Sales and Head of Sales Enablement personas care about (pipeline, quota attainment, rep ramp time -- not abstract UX principles), and specifies conversion rate as the primary metric -- without a testing recommendation, the variants are creative options with no execution plan
